criteria: The agent has read the Introduction completely to understand the motivation for structure-agnostic, composition-only property prediction and why composition-weighted CBFV approaches lose chemical signal from low-fraction (dopant) elements.

The agent has read the 'Data and materials properties procurement' section to understand the Matbench/Extended dataset sources, the fivefold-CV vs. fixed-seed splitting schemes, and the duplicate-composition handling rules.

The agent has read the 'Benchmark comparisons' section to understand the MAE-based comparison of Roost, CrabNet, HotCrab, ElemNet, and RF, and why only two Matbench properties are compared against Automatminer.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the full list of 28 benchmark datasets, their sources, target properties, sample counts, and train/val/test split proportions.

The agent has read the Methods section 'Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture' to understand the EDM featurization scheme, mat2vec vs. one-hot element representations, and the dual sine/cosine fractional embedding.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The agent has read Table 2 together with Figs. 6-8 to understand the exact default architecture hyperparameters and the data flow through the attention blocks and residual network.
The agent has read the 'Training CrabNet' section to understand the optimizer, cyclical learning-rate schedule, batch-size logic, random seed, and hardware used for all training runs.
The agent has read the 'Reference models' section to understand how Roost, ElemNet, and the RF baseline were configured and adapted for benchmarking.
The agent has read the 'Visualizing self-attention' section and Figs. 2-5 to understand the four interpretability analyses enabled by CrabNet's attention-based design.

The agent has read the Data Availability and Code Availability statements to understand that pre-split benchmark data, the CrabNet source code, and pre-trained weights are hosted on the cited GitHub/Zenodo repositories.
The plan includes collecting and combining the 10 Matbench datasets and 18 Extended datasets into the full set of 28 benchmark properties.
The plan includes splitting the Matbench datasets using fivefold cross-validation as defined in the original Automatminer publication.
The plan includes splitting the Extended datasets into train, validation, and test sets using a fixed random seed.

The plan includes the post-split deduplication procedure: removing duplicate compositions within each split, and removing any validation/test compound whose composition already exists in the train or validation split.
The plan includes the dataset-specific duplicate-resolution rule: keeping the lowest-formation-enthalpy entry for OQMD/MP datasets and the mean target value for all other datasets.
The plan includes implementing the EDM (element-derived matrix) featurization as the element-wise sum of an element embedding matrix and a fractional embedding matrix.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The plan includes using mat2vec embeddings as the default chemical element representation, with a separate one-hot atomic-number representation for the HotCrab ablation.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The plan includes the dual sine/cosine fractional embedding consisting of a linear-scale component and a logarithmic-scale component, inspired by Transformer positional encoding, to preserve both ordinary and dopant-level stoichiometry.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The plan includes a Transformer encoder with N=3 stacked self-attention layers and H=4 attention heads per layer, matching Table 2.

The plan includes a residual output network mapping each element's final representation to an element-proto-contribution, element-uncertainty, and element-logit, using hidden layer sizes [1024, 512, 256, 128].

The plan includes the prediction logic of scaling proto-contributions by a sigmoid-gated logit and averaging the resulting element contributions (and element-uncertainties) across all elements to obtain the compound-level prediction and aleatoric uncertainty.

The plan includes the training procedure: a Lookahead-wrapped Lamb optimizer, a cyclical learning rate between 1e-4 and 6e-3 every 4 epochs, a robust MAE loss, and a fixed random seed of 42 for every run.
The plan includes dynamically sized training batches bounded to the range [2^7, 2^12] and a fixed inference batch size of 2^7.
The plan includes benchmarking CrabNet against Roost, ElemNet, and a Magpie-CBFV random forest, each run with its published default settings on all 28 datasets.
The plan includes evaluating all models with the MAE metric (Equation 1) only after training and hyperparameter tuning are complete, using a strictly held-out test set.
comment: Equation 1; Data and materials properties procurement

criteria: The plan includes the four self-attention interpretability analyses: per-head attention matrices for a single composition, dataset-averaged per-element attention, element-contribution maps, and composition-sweep predictions for a binary A_xB_(1-x) system.
The code parses chemical formulas into constituent atomic numbers and their fractional (stoichiometric) amounts as the raw model input representation.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code implements the mat2vec embedding lookup as the default element representation, with input dimension d_in=200.
The code implements a one-hot atomic-number element representation (d_in=118) used for the HotCrab variant.
The code implements a learned fully connected embedding network that projects the chosen element representation into the d_model=512 attention input space.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code implements the fractional embedding's linear-scale sine/cosine projection of normalized fractional amounts with a fractional resolution of 0.01.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code implements the fractional embedding's logarithmic-scale sine/cosine projection that preserves very small fractional amounts (down to roughly 1e-6), enabling representation of dopant-level stoichiometry.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code concatenates the linear-scale and log-scale fractional embedding halves (each of dimension d_model/2) across the embedding dimension to form the full fractional embedding matrix.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code computes the EDM as the element-wise sum of the element embedding matrix and the fractional embedding matrix for each composition.
The code zero-pads EDM rows for compositions with fewer elements than the maximum number of element rows (inferred from the largest composition in the dataset/batch).
The code batches per-composition EDM slices into a 3D tensor of shape (n_compounds, n_elements, d_model) for input to the Transformer encoder.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code implements three independent fully connected projections (FC_Q, FC_K, FC_V) that generate the Query, Key, and Value tensors from the EDM.
The code implements scaled dot-product self-attention, computing softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) and multiplying by V to produce the updated element representation Z.
The code implements H=4 independent attention heads per layer, each computing its own Q_h, K_h, V_h, and Z_h tensors.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code concatenates the per-head Z_h tensors along the feature dimension and passes the result through a combining fully connected layer to produce the updated EDM' for the layer.
The code stacks N=3 self-attention blocks (Transformer encoder layers) that iteratively refine element representations into the final EDM''.
comment: Methods: Self-attention and the CrabNet architecture

criteria: The code implements the residual output network with hidden layer sizes [1024, 512, 256, 128] that maps each element's final representation (EDM'') to a 3-channel output.
The code interprets the residual network's three output channels per element as the element-proto-contribution p', element-uncertainty u', and element-logit, respectively.
The code computes the element scaling factor s as the sigmoid of the element-logit.
The code computes each element's contribution y' as the product of its proto-contribution p' and its scaling factor s.
The code computes the final per-compound property prediction as the mean of the element contributions y' across all elements present in the composition.
The code computes the aleatoric uncertainty prediction as the mean of the element-uncertainties u' across all elements in the composition.
The code configures the default CrabNet hyperparameters to match Table 2: d_model=512, d_ff=2048, d_k=d_q=128, H=4, N=3, d_out=3.
The code implements PyTorch Dataset and DataLoader classes to load and batch the featurized EDM data for training and inference.
The code scales target property values to zero mean and unit variance for training and inverts this scaling before computing evaluation metrics.
The code dynamically computes the training batch size from the training-set size, clipped to the range [2^7, 2^12], and fixes the inference batch size at 2^7.
The code wraps the Lamb optimizer with the Lookahead mechanism for updating model weights.
The code implements a cyclical learning-rate schedule oscillating between 1e-4 and 6e-3 with a 4-epoch cycle length.
The code implements the robust MAE loss criterion (adopted from Roost) used to train CrabNet.
The code fixes the random seed to 42 for every training run across all 28 materials properties.
The code implements data loading and preprocessing for the 10 Matbench properties using the fivefold cross-validation splits matching Automatminer.
The code implements data loading and preprocessing for the 18 Extended-dataset properties using fixed-seed train/validation/test splits.

The code implements duplicate-composition filtering applied after splitting, removing within-split duplicates and any validation/test compound whose composition already appears in train or validation.
The code implements the OQMD/MP-specific duplicate-resolution rule that retains the entry with the lowest formation enthalpy among duplicate compositions.
The code implements the mean-target duplicate-resolution rule for duplicate compositions in all non-OQMD/MP datasets.
The code implements the MAE metric (Equation 1) used to evaluate and compare all benchmarked models.
The code integrates the public Roost repository, with the minor adaptations needed to automate training and benchmarking across all 28 properties.
The code integrates the public ElemNet repository with custom scripts to train and evaluate it with default parameters across all 28 properties.
The code implements the random-forest baseline using a Magpie CBFV (via Matminer) with scikit-learn's RandomForestRegressor at n_estimators=500 and otherwise default parameters.
The code ensures training and validation data are used only for fitting and hyperparameter tuning, with the held-out test set used exclusively for final performance evaluation.
The code consumes pre-split, cleaned CSV files for each of the 28 datasets to guarantee that data splits exactly match those used to generate the paper's reported results.
The code extracts and visualizes per-head attention matrices for a single composition (e.g., Al2O3) from a trained CrabNet model.
The code computes and visualizes the dataset-averaged per-element attention (e.g., Si's average attention across all Si-containing compounds) for each attention head.
The code extracts and visualizes average per-element contributions to a target property on a periodic table layout and as per-element contribution distributions.

The code implements a composition-sweep prediction utility that generates predictions, element contributions, and aleatoric uncertainty for a binary system A_xB_(1-x) across a swept range of x values.
Default CrabNet (mat2vec) has been trained and evaluated successfully on all 28 benchmark datasets without errors.
HotCrab (one-hot CrabNet variant) has been trained and evaluated successfully on all 28 benchmark datasets without errors.
Roost has been trained and evaluated successfully on all 28 benchmark datasets without errors.
ElemNet has been trained and evaluated on all 28 benchmark datasets, including the expected convergence failures (NaN outputs) on properties such as phonons and steels_yield.
The Magpie-CBFV random forest baseline has been trained and evaluated successfully on all 28 benchmark datasets without errors.
The attention and element-contribution visualization scripts have been executed successfully to reproduce the figures corresponding to Figs. 2-5.

The execution outcomes match Fig. 1a: on the Matbench properties, default CrabNet achieves the best or near-best MAE among Roost/CrabNet/HotCrab/ElemNet/RF on most properties (e.g., Castelli perovskites MAE ~0.127, experimental band gap MAE ~0.338).

The execution outcomes match Fig. 1b: on the Extended dataset properties, CrabNet and Roost achieve comparable MAEs that are consistently better than ElemNet and RF (e.g., AFLOW bulk modulus MAE ~8.7-8.8 GPa).
The execution outcomes confirm that Roost and both CrabNet variants achieve substantially lower MAE than ElemNet and RF on nearly all 28 benchmark properties.

The execution outcomes confirm that mat2vec-based CrabNet outperforms the one-hot HotCrab variant on most properties, except AFLOW thermal conductivity, MP elastic anisotropy, and the largest (OQMD) datasets.
The execution outcomes reproduce ElemNet's convergence failures (NaN MAE) on the phonons and steels_yield properties.
The execution outcomes match the reported CrabNet vs. Automatminer comparison on expt_gap, with CrabNet's MAE (~0.338-0.416 eV depending on run) better than Automatminer's reported 0.416 eV.
The execution outcomes match the reported CrabNet vs. Automatminer comparison on steels_yield, with CrabNet's MAE (~91.7-92 GPa) better than Automatminer's reported 95.2 GPa.

The execution outcomes confirm CrabNet outperforms the RF baseline on the data-poor steels_yield task (312 samples, up to 13 elements per composition), despite RF's typical advantage in low-data regimes.

The execution outcomes are consistent with the paper's claim that CrabNet's validation-tuned default hyperparameters do not give it a decisive unfair advantage, given its consistently strong performance across all 28 properties.

The execution outcomes match Fig. 2: visualizing per-head attention matrices for Al2O3 shows Al and O attending strongly to each other in at least one head (e.g., an attention score near 1.00 between Al and O).
The execution outcomes match Fig. 3: the averaged attention from Si in the aflow__Egap-trained CrabNet model shows each of the 4 attention heads exhibiting distinct, non-redundant element groupings.
The execution outcomes match Fig. 4: the AFLOW bulk-modulus element-contribution analysis shows lithium contributing little and tungsten contributing substantially to predicted bulk modulus.
The execution outcomes match Fig. 5: predicted band gap for the Si_xO_(1-x) system shows a transition between conducting and semiconducting behavior between x=0.5 and x=0.7.
The execution outcomes confirm the sample counts and split proportions for all 28 benchmark properties match Table 1.
